title: Embedded Power Profiler @Withings
description: Real-time power consumption analysis toolchain for IoT healthcare devices (smart watches, smart scales), in production at Withings.
markdown:
## What
A real-time power consumption analysis toolchain for Withings' connected health devices, processing up to 13 billion data points per day from dual ADCs to identify firmware bugs and power inefficiencies. Enables engineers to validate battery life optimisations critical for products like ScanWatch (30-day battery vs Apple Watch's 18 hours).

## How
Reverse-engineered a proprietary Ethernet protocol running over a non-standard EtherType from 2017 internal documentation and firmware source: a mixed byte stream encoding two ADCs (ADS1247 24-bit @ 2kHz, LTC2378-18 18-bit @ 62.5kHz) with an implicit upsampling scheme via pipe markers used to synchronise sampling rates. Designed a custom binary storage format using cumulative sum encoding, reducing range average calculation from O(n) to O(1) and enabling constant-time downsampling of arbitrarily large acquisitions directly from disk. Prototyped file I/O performance in C++ and Rust before settling on Python with numpy for the final implementation. Built a Qt/PyQtGraph GUI replacing a 46-class legacy Tkinter/matplotlib system with a streamlined 10-class MVC architecture, achieving smooth real-time rendering of billions of points downsampled to screen pixel width.

## Result
Unlimited acquisition length (24+ hours, 13 billion points per day), 500 KB/s sustained streaming, and fluid real-time visualisation regardless of dataset size. Successfully deployed to production, catching critical firmware bugs including a weight-on-scale state machine issue causing excess power consumption. Used across embedded software, industrialisation and SQA teams for pre-production power profiling.

### AI Usage
Spent 4 months deliberately learning Qt, PyQtGraph and the embedded hardware stack. AI assisted with documentation generation, debugging edge cases and test case creation. Core engineering decisions were manual: cumulative sum storage design, protocol reverse-engineering, dual-ADC synchronisation logic and architecture simplification.
